# Proof that the Usual Topology on the Reals Equals the Order Topology

**Proposition.** The usual topology for $\mathbb{R}$ is identical with the order topology. The usual topology has a countable base.

*Proof.* The usual topology on $\mathbb{R}$ is generated by the metric $d(x,y) = |x-y|$, with basic open sets being open intervals $(a,b) = \{x : a < x < b\}$.

The order topology on $\mathbb{R}$ has as a subbase the sets $\{x : x < a\}$ and $\{x : x > a\}$ for each $a \in \mathbb{R}$. Finite intersections of these subbasic sets yield open intervals:
$$\{x : x < b\} \cap \{x : x > a\} = (a,b).$$
Thus the order topology is generated by open intervals $(a,b)$ as well.

Since both topologies have exactly the same base (the collection of all open intervals), they are identical.

For the second claim: the collection of intervals with rational endpoints,
$$\mathcal{B} = \{(p,q) : p, q \in \mathbb{Q}, p < q\},$$
is a countable base for the usual topology. Indeed, $\mathbb{Q} \times \mathbb{Q}$ is countable, and for any open interval $(a,b)$ and any $x \in (a,b)$, there exist rational numbers $p, q$ with $a < p < x < q < b$, giving $x \in (p,q) \subseteq (a,b)$. $\square$
